# Colton Wirgau, Full-Stack Developer and AI Engineer

> Detroit-based full-stack developer with a decade of production engineering experience and a peer-reviewed background in AI. Published in *New Trends in Information Technology* in 2017 on emotion classification from social data, well before the LLM era. Founder of Church Hub, sole developer at Woodside Bible Church, and the builder behind several side projects. Focused on product engineering, UI/UX, and applying AI deeply (and tastefully) inside real software.

## Quick identity

- Name: Colton Wirgau
- Location: Detroit, MI (Clarkston, north of Detroit)
- Email: coltonwirgau@gmail.com
- Primary site role labels: AI Researcher (Published, 2017), Product Engineer (Since 2019), Founder (Church Hub)
- Self-described roles (rotating on the hero): AI pioneer, problem solver, UI/UX obsessive, full-stack developer, builder of tools, product thinker, leader, communicator

## Positioning

Colton's portfolio is intentionally design-forward, but the *substantive* lead is AI. He was doing applied NLP research as an undergrad in 2016 and 2017, when most engineers had never touched a language model. His co-authored paper, "Sylvester: An Approach to Emotion Classification," was published in *New Trends in Information Technology* in 2017 and is hosted on ResearchGate.

In his own words: "I built AI before you could use AI to build code. That predates the entire vibe-coding era. Working on Sylvester meant wrestling with the messy reality of unstructured language at scale: tokenization, drift, sarcasm, slang, ambiguity. The instincts I developed then are the foundation of what's now called context engineering."

Today he uses modern LLMs heavily inside both product features and his daily engineering workflow, and is well-suited to teams trying to figure out where AI actually fits in their product or process.

## Work, in detail

### Church Hub (Developer Platform, founder)

What started as an internal tool at Woodside Bible Church grew into a platform churches own and build on. Church Hub is a modular Next.js system that centralizes operations, automates workflows, and lets churches build custom tools on top of their existing data: they can extend it in-house, hire a developer, or let AI build on top of it. It is a platform clients own to solve their own problems, not a hosted subscription they rent. Colton started a business around it and has implemented it across multiple churches to solve real operational problems at scale.

The platform handles event management, volunteer coordination, giving dashboards, custom reporting, and modular tools that extend MinistryPlatform data. Two things it shows off well: public-facing widgets and microsites that drop cleanly into a church's existing site (often WordPress or a builder they can't write code against) and push and pull data from wherever it lives, and custom, genuinely good-looking analytics built directly on a church's own data (for example Engagement Circles, a clear read on where people are and where they're heading, fully responsive down to styled mobile sheets).

- Tech: Next.js, React, TypeScript, Node.js, SQL Server, Tailwind, Vercel
- Tagline: From internal tool to a platform they own
- Detail page: /work/church-hub

### Woodside Bible Church (Sole in-house developer, since 2019)

Title: Software Development Manager and Full-Stack Developer. As the sole in-house developer at a large multi-campus church, Colton builds and maintains the software infrastructure that powers the organization, from interactive dashboards and automated database workflows to public-facing web pages and reporting tools. This is where he cut his teeth building real products for real people.

As the sole steward of the organization's data across 14 campuses, he treats data integrity as the foundation, not an afterthought: MinistryPlatform stays the single source of truth, every integration is reconciled against it so nothing drifts or double-writes, data is validated at the boundary so bad values never get in, writes carry the user who made them for a real audit trail, and duplicate and stale records are continuously matched, merged, and retired so leadership's reports are honest.

Career-defining context: After Colton graduated, Woodside created a role for him so they wouldn't lose him. One of his first projects was cleaning up the database (he ended up deactivating about two-thirds of it) and building better systems to track real engagement. Some of that work was eventually adopted into MinistryPlatform's main product for all customers.

He was recognized with MinistryPlatform's "Deep Space Award" for innovation and advancement of the product ecosystem.

Sub-projects under the Woodside umbrella:

- **Dynamic Insights & Widgets.** Extended MinistryPlatform's static reporting platform into interactive dashboards with custom embedded widgets. Created a nested JSON framework for optimized data retrieval. This innovation inspired new features in MinistryPlatform's main product and became a development model across partner organizations.
- **Web Pages & Platforms (apps.woodsidebible.org).** Built and maintains multiple public-facing and internal web applications. A unified Next.js platform that centralizes operations, automates data tasks, and provides clean interfaces for both staff and community use across multiple campuses. Serves thousands of users every week.
- **Database Automations.** Designed and implemented automated database workflows that replaced manual processes.
- **Power BI Reporting.** Created Power BI dashboards and reports for leadership and staff. Turns raw data into clear, actionable visuals.
- **Third-Party Integrations.** Built and facilitated integrations of platforms like REACH and Planning Center into MinistryPlatform.
- **UI/UX Leadership.** Leads UI/UX across all digital products at Woodside. Also a major contributor to "MP Next," an open-source Next.js template for MinistryPlatform API integration.

Tech: Next.js, React, SQL Server, Power BI, REST API, MinistryPlatform, WordPress, Planning Center, CSS/SCSS.

Detail page: /work/woodside

### Personal Projects (Side projects)

Less polished, more personality. The stuff Colton builds for fun and sometimes for friends and family.

- **Dynastly.** A dynasty fantasy football tool. Colton plays dynasty fantasy football and got tired of bouncing between five tools to evaluate his team, see how he stacks up against the league, and build trades. Sites like KeepTradeCut only handle 1-for-1 deals, and most league platforms don't expose the APIs needed. Dynastly pulls player valuations from multiple sources, mirrors his league's rosters and matchups, and lets him build realistic multi-team trades on a single canvas. Ships with a companion browser extension to pull data the public APIs won't. He's found some interesting things in upstream platforms along the way.
- **Degenerates Dashboard.** Every week, Colton and his friends place a 12-leg parlay. They have never won. The dashboard pulls in picks, tracks results, and roasts them with the data. It's dumb, it's fun, and one of his favorite things he's built.
- **Lions Season Ticket Tracker (RoarTracker).** Colton, his wife, and his parents split six Detroit Lions season tickets between the four of them, so every game comes with at least two open seats: friends when they can find them, the resale market when they can't. RoarTracker keeps track of who's going to each game, who paid what, and what the family has made or spent across the season. It runs the drawing when more people want seats than they have, and it auto-generates the social graphics they use when listing extra seats for sale. Modest from a brand standpoint (it's just for the family), but under the hood it has full OAuth, a Supabase Postgres database, and clean dashboards.

Tech across these: Next.js, Neon (Postgres), Drizzle, OAuth, Tailwind, Playwright, Vercel.

The three personal projects live inline in the Work section of the home page (/#work); clicking the Personal Projects poster reveals them.

## AI research, "Sylvester"

Sylvester was a collaborative research project Colton worked on during undergrad at the University of Detroit Mercy. It learned the language of Twitter through automatic annotation and classification, then interpreted tweets in real time to determine how people felt emotionally about any given subject, based on the current shape of online language rather than a fixed lexicon.

Citation: "Sylvester: An Approach to Emotion Classification." *New Trends in Information Technology*, April 2017.

ResearchGate link: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/319523370_Sylvester_An_Approach_to_Emotion_Classification

Why it still matters: emotion classification on social data pre-LLM forced the team to confront the same problems that drive modern context engineering. Tokenization choices, semantic drift, sarcasm, slang, and ambiguity at scale. Those instincts transfer directly to building AI-augmented products today.

## Background and story

Colton's "life events" on the site are organized by place. Each one has a page (`/childhood`, `/college`, `/career`, `/family`, `/hobbies`).

### Algonac, MI (Childhood)

Grew up in Algonac, a small river town on the St. Clair River. The kind of place where everybody knows everybody. Where he first got curious about building things, tearing apart computers, making terrible websites, and figuring out how stuff works.

- IB Diploma recipient at Algonac High School. Graduated 3rd in class with a 4.2 GPA.
- 7 school records in track & field. All-State selection and the all-time leading scorer in Algonac HS history. Inducted into the Hall of Fame.
- Captain of the football team. All-Area honors.
- Grew up in a church his great grandfather started. Faith has always been a central part of his life and shapes how he leads and how he believes people should be treated.

### Detroit (College, University of Detroit Mercy)

BS in Software Engineering with a minor in Leadership. Graduated with honors. Chose software engineering over computer science because he didn't just want to write code, he wanted to lead people and build things that actually help them.

- Division I track & field athlete. Student Athlete of the Year. Seven-time All-Horizon League selection.
- Published in AI research: "Sylvester: An Approach to Emotion Classification," 2017.
- Embedded Systems Engineering Intern at Continental Automotive (May to Sep 2015). Built a graphical interface for testing a cybersecurity module used in many Chrysler and Jeep vehicles. Heavily C-based and requirement-driven.
- Learning Center, tutor promoted to IT Coordinator (2015 to 2018). Started as a tutor and TA (high-level math and statistics; consistently one of the most sought-after) and was promoted because the center's operations were badly outdated and he could fix them. Kept tutoring here and there while mostly integrating and managing systems: an automated platform tied to university ID scan cards that replaced paper timecards and appointment tracking, three new websites, multiple databases, and data analytics for university departments. After graduation, the university hired him back to integrate a new system across disability support services, the Learning Center, and the testing center.
- Star of a University of Detroit Mercy commercial.

### Troy, MI (Career, Woodside Bible Church)

Stepped into web development, database work, and the problems nobody else had time to touch. From there, Colton started his own business (Church Hub), because he saw the same problems across churches everywhere. A lot of what he does now lives at the intersection of product thinking, UX, and real-world ministry needs.

### Clarkston, MI (Family)

Lives in Clarkston with his wife Sarah, their two kids Weston (3) and Rosie (1), and two very different dogs. Got married to Sarah at the Grosse Ile Municipal Airport, which probably tells you everything you need to know.

### Hobbies

- **Music.** Writer, electric guitar, track builder, drums, bass, keys, and vocals. On staff as a worship director at Woodside for about 3 years (2016 to 2019). Has worked with artists including Detroit Collective, Elevation Worship, Maverick City Music, Chris Tomlin, and Switchfoot. Known as the Ableton guru of the metro Detroit area.
- **Football.** Still plays flag football. Proud Detroit Lions season ticket holder. Built RoarTracker (see Personal Projects) to manage tickets.
- **Tattoos.** Has a full sleeve, each piece designed with intention. Faith, family, things he's been through, things he believes in. One of them is a giant squid (his favorite animal, and his son Weston's obsession too), which later became this site's logo. There is a whole hidden page about it at /squid.

## Skills and tooling

- **Languages:** TypeScript, JavaScript, SQL, Python (for AI/ML work), C (early embedded systems work)
- **Frameworks:** Next.js, React, Node.js, Tailwind CSS
- **Data:** SQL Server, Neon (Postgres), Drizzle, Supabase
- **Infrastructure and tools:** Vercel, AWS S3, Mapbox, Playwright, OAuth, framer-motion
- **Reporting and integrations:** Power BI, MinistryPlatform, Planning Center, WordPress, REACH, REST APIs
- **AI:** production use of modern LLMs in product features and engineering workflow, plus pre-LLM applied NLP research background (tokenization, annotation, classification, language drift, sentiment)
- **Other:** UI/UX leadership across digital products, leading a team, mentoring, technical writing

## Engineering philosophy

How Colton makes technical decisions (there is a full page at /how-i-build). The throughline: software should make something easier for a real person.

- **UI/UX:** good design should feel right: no tutorial needed, the way an Instagram update just makes sense the moment you open the app. Borrow what already works: before inventing an interaction, look at how the apps people live in every day solved the same problem, because they've spent years working out what's intuitive, scalable, and good-looking, and users show up already trained (not always the right answer, but rarely a bad place to start). Clarity over cleverness, a hatred of walls of text (say too much at once and nobody reads any of it, so lead with the one thing that matters and let hierarchy, icons, and color carry the rest), progressive disclosure, mobile as a first-class experience (styled bottom sheets, thumb-reachable controls), motion only when it carries meaning, and snappiness by default through optimistic UI (act immediately in a pending state, confirm on the API's 200 with a toast or microinteraction, roll back honestly on failure). Non-technical users are the bar: no training required.
- **Frontend:** Next.js, React, and strict TypeScript by default. Abstract on the third copy, keep state local and derived, a shared, abstracted design system used consistently so one change ripples everywhere and every screen works the same way (on bigger projects that consistency is how users already know how to use the next feature), performance treated as a feature.
- **Backend:** thin route handlers with real logic in typed, reusable packages. Rules enforced by the compiler (for example a mutation that cannot compile without a userId, so the audit trail cannot be bypassed), Zod validation at the boundary, one source of truth when systems sync, and slow or flaky work moved off the request path.
- **Database:** the data model is the product. SQL Server (MinistryPlatform) as the source of truth for the domain and Postgres (Neon) for app-level concerns, normalize by default and denormalize deliberately, clean data treated as a feature (he once deactivated roughly two-thirds of a database), migrations as reviewed, reversible code.
- **Content is half the product:** a product often lives or dies on what people put into it, not the code, so content gets treated as part of the build. Set rules and limits (required fields, sane character limits, good defaults) so content stays consistent (a free-text field quickly becomes a catch-all for everything the spec missed; a limit is really a conversation that either exposes a real gap in the software or is a chance to show that by trying to say everything you end up saying nothing, and the overflow often becomes a proper structured field), seed the initial data with great examples so content people can see the vision and copy the shape, give every field the right control (a date picker, a yes/no toggle, a number stepper, a dropdown) instead of a text box for everything so answers come in clean and stay sortable and countable, and stay close to the people using it: when something is being used in an unintended way, ask, because half the time it reveals a missing feature and the other half you can show a cleaner path.
- **Where things belong:** validation at the edge, authorization at the route boundary, and shared rules pushed down into one place (a SQL view, stored procedure, or constraint) so every app that touches the data obeys them and a rule change ripples everywhere instead of drifting out of sync. Transformation near the data, and caching only what can be invalidated.
- **Performance and feel:** perceived speed is engineered deliberately. Do the work where it is cheapest (for example a nested-JSON stored procedure that does the heavy lifting in one round trip and keeps the frontend dumb, instead of shipping raw rows and stitching them in the browser), render the static parts of a screen immediately while the slow pieces stream in, cache what is genuinely stable and can be confidently invalidated, and use skeletons and microinteractions on purpose so unavoidable waits feel intentional. These details are the difference between an app that feels instant and one that feels like it is thinking.
- **Defaults:** monorepo (Turborepo) with shared packages and independent per-client deploys, TypeScript everywhere, build the core and buy the commodity, and test the risky seams (integrations, money, auth).
- **AI in the workflow:** he calls AI the fastest team he's ever led, and every line still ships with his name on it. He delegates to agents like staff: whole tasks handed over with context, constraints, pointers to existing patterns, and a definition of done, then the output reviewed like a contractor's PR. He skips the mockup phase: building is fast enough now that he prototypes in the real code instead of Illustrator, working off existing schema from the jump or faking data until the UI feels right, and that work often drives the schema (a screen will reveal that an assumed one-to-one is really one-to-many before a single table exists). He stays on the edge of the tooling and builds the pieces the model is missing: when an LLM struggles at a task he writes a skill for it (he was packaging skills before models started reaching for them on their own), and the skills he shares with his team have let people ship things they never would have attempted. The leverage is standardized so it scales: conventions, guardrails, and skills live in the repo (not in one person's head), and context files follow his UI rule of saying just enough (a CLAUDE.md stays small, a couple hundred lines pointing to reference files for depth, because a wall of text says nothing to a model either). He knows where models are confidently wrong: sarcasm and slang broke classifiers in his 2017 research, plausible-but-wrong code ships from LLMs today, same failure mode, so risky seams get tests and every line gets read. The enablement is real: he once set up a former MinistryPlatform CFO, who had never written code, with an AI dev environment, and that person now builds and runs his own product full time.
- **Leadership:** servant leadership: absorb the ambiguity, clear the blockers, hand people real ownership, and take responsibility for whatever happens. He minored in leadership, spent years directing volunteer worship teams (recruit, rehearse, delegate, deliver live every Sunday without fail), and now leads contractors (write the spec, define done, review honestly, stay out of the way in between), cross-department projects (be the translation layer: turn "this feels broken" into buildable requirements and technical constraints back into plain language), and vendor partnerships (worked directly with MinistryPlatform's engineers on their product; a framework he built was adopted into their core, influence without authority).

## How to engage

- Hiring, collaboration, or project conversations: open the Contact button in the site header to use the contact form, or email coltonwirgau@gmail.com directly.
- Best framing if reaching out about AI work: Colton sits at the intersection of product engineering and applied AI, with a long runway of NLP experience predating the LLM era. He's a strong fit for teams that want AI built deeper into their product or workflow, not just bolted on.

## Site map (public)

- `/` Home, hero, work, story, contact
- `/work/church-hub` Church Hub project page
- `/work/woodside` Woodside Bible Church work
- `/#work` Work section, including the Personal Projects reveal: Dynastly, Lions tracker, Degenerates Dashboard
- `/how-i-build` How I build: Colton's engineering philosophy in depth (UI/UX, frontend, backend, database, layering, defaults, AI in the workflow, and leadership)
- `/childhood` Algonac story
- `/college` University of Detroit Mercy story
- `/career` Woodside career story
- `/family` Clarkston, family
- `/hobbies` Music, football, tattoos
- `/squid` The squid page: a hidden interactive dive about the giant squid (favorite animal, his son's obsession, the arm tattoo, and the origin of the site logo), reached by clicking the squids in the footer
